paragraphs.text: . The beach at the landing-place was strewn with goods of every description. In no other day this year has so much merchandise been unladen here. Though
exhibits no improvement in number of buildings or increase in population, still its trade has augmented very considerably. The valleys back of, and surrounding it, has

alone as their mart, and yearly the wants of the native Californians become greater and more extravagant. Though they refuse to adopt some of our labor-saving inventions, they show a disposition rivalius [sic] in dress and imitate us in many of our habits. In horseback exercises they certainly have nothing to learn, but in their vehicles it would be difficult for any people to be more clumsy; and yet they steadily decline substituting our style of carts or wagons instead. Occasionally one of the wealthier classes indulges in the use of a carriage, and when they do, invariably they choose the oldest and clumsiest style.
A short time since I called to see the
, an old trapper, who has been on
for the last eighteen years. She was brought by
, seventy miles distant, and not, as before published, from the
No person has yet been found that can speak or understand her language.
was killed and torn to pieces by the wild dogs with which

Her only dress was a sort of gown, sitting high upon the shoulders and reaching nearly to the knees, made of the skins and feathers of wild ducks, sewed together with the sinews of the seal. The handicraft shown in the manufacture of her needles, sewing stuff, baskets, water vessels—to make all of which a piece of the blade of an old knife seems to have been her only tool—is certainly curious. The water jugs are made of split sea-grass woven tightly together in flask form, and the borrom and part of the sides daubed over with asphaltum, springs of which are on
. She has a piece of netting made of sinews, some eight feet square, the knitting of which is precisely similar to that made for fishing.
Her food seems to have been chiefly the fat of the seal. Of this she brought some twenty pounds with her. It is rancid and smells awfully; still she relishes it.
She is very fond of shell-fish, coffee, and liquor of every sort; but does not care for beef, pork, bread, nor tea.
In person she is by far the best looking Indian, male or female, that I have ever seen on

. She has a large, full eye; her forehead is low and broad; her nose slightly aquiline and finely formed; her mouth is rather large, and indicates great firmness; he protruding lower lip gives a haughty tone to her look, and her well set chin fully sustains this trait. She is of medium stature, very masculine in appearance, and shows very little evidence of advanced age.
Undoubtedly she is the last of
, arrived here, after an absence of thirteen years.
in 1825, and married into one of the wealthy native families. He is father-in-law to
, one of the heaviest merchants south of
, and universally known for his princely hospitality.
The troubles which threatened some time back in reference with Squatters, are nearly subsided, and a very good feeling exists between the American and native population.
I was shown to-day some specimens of quartz gold and copper ore taken from a spot about fifty miles distant. Both are very rich.
fell from his horse yesterday, while riding in company with
, and sustained severe, though not serious injury to his right ankle.
The weather here for a few days past has been excessively hot, owing to the great fires in the mountains in the vicinity.
Should any of the good people of
ever deem in worth their while to visit
, let me advise them not to leave it without visiting
, or rather what remains of it.
is now one of the finest in

in exterior appearance, and internally it is the best adorned and most tastefully arranged that I have yet seen. It contains eleven large paintings and about twenty small ones, all of them excellently executed—indeed, a few are chef d'ouvres. The ruins of
what was once a large Mission settlement
lie scattered in every direction about

. Especially interesting amoung these ruins are the almost complete vestiges of a viaduct, reservoir, mill, and holy bathing houses. The walls, arches, cement and stone-work of each of these are in good condition; but they now stand merely the evidence of former use. A
of the Franciscan order officiates at
